content:
John 8:3-9
• What caused the accusers to go away “one by one, beginning with the older ones”? What would have been the accusers’ view of themselves as they came? What would have been their view as they left? What did they miss?
• How did the teachers of the law and the Pharisees treat the woman caught in adultery? How did Jesus treat her?  What can I learn about Jesus from this? How does this give me courage to come to Jesus with my sins?
John 8:10-11
• How have I personally experienced the words “Neither do I condemn you; go, and from now on sin no more”?

John 8:12-13
• Reflect on Jesus’ words: “I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.”
• How has following Jesus led me out of darkness and into the “light of life”?
John 8:23-28
• Reflect on the anguished heart with which Jesus would have spoken v. 24: “I told you that you would die in your sins, for unless you believe that I am he you will die in your sins.”
• What about the cross (where Jesus was “lifted up”) causes people to know that Jesus is indeed the one he claimed to be (v. 28)?

John 8:31-36
• Most people would say, as the people did in v. 33, “We…have never been enslaved to anyone.” In what ways are people today enslaved?
• How has Jesus set me free from being “a slave to sin”?
John 8:37, 43-44, 47
• Jesus is addressing those who have already decided to kill him. What is the reason for their rejection of Jesus?  How does Jesus’ warning—that the issue is not a lack of clarity but their hostile relationship with truth—apply today? Are there times I have found spiritual issues confusing because my desires have clouded the truth?
John 8:44-46
• Consider the sobering statement here about lying. How am I challenged about my speech?

John 8:55
• From the two ways Jesus describes his relationship with the Father, what can I learn about what constitutes a relationship with God? Contrast this to the Jews’ notion of what it meant to belong to God.
John 8:48-59
• The ongoing battle between Jesus and the Jews throughout this chapter boils down to the issue of who Jesus is and who they are. What are their differing answers to these two questions?
• What is the significance of Jesus’ claim in v. 58? Where do I stand on this question of Jesus’ identity?
Again Jesus spoke to them, saying, “I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.”

John 9:2-3
• What kind of worldview is represented by the disciples’ question in v. 2? Are there ways I can relate to this view of sin, God, and life’s circumstances?
• How does Jesus’ response clarify this issue? How can I respond to painful aspects of my past or my life today so that “the works of God might be displayed” in my life?
John 9:4-5
• What is the significance of these phrases: “while it is day” and “night is coming”? What are some windows of opportunity currently open to me? In what ways, and to whom, can I bring healing and Jesus’ light?
John 9:6-7
• What can I learn about obedience and blessing from the fact that this man obeyed despite his limited knowledge?

John 9:8-17
• After the healing, the man finds himself alienated from his neighbors and in trouble with the authorities. What can we make of this turn of events?
• What implication does this have on what a new Christian can expect from those who knew him before?
John 9:18-23
• In what ways is this scene of the parents’ interrogation reflective of our world today where it’s “already agreed that if anyone should confess Jesus to be Christ” they would “be put out” of the circle? What are some examples of such circles?
• Is there some way I can relate to the parents’ noncommittal response?

John 9:11-38
• What is the progression of how the man refers to Jesus (vv. 11, 17, 33, 38)?
• How have I experienced this kind of progression in my understanding of who God is throughout my spiritual journey?
• How did the harsh interrogation of the man and his vigorous defense of Jesus before the Pharisees play a role in strengthening his faith? What are some lessons for me?
• How does the man manage to remain unshaken by the religious leaders’ intimidation and interrogation? How am I challenged by the man’s clear and simple testimony (v. 25)? What can I learn about the power of personal witness?

John 10:3-5
• Reflect on the words “the sheep follow him, for they know his voice.” How have I grown to recognize Jesus’ voice in my life?
• What are the other voices that might muffle Jesus’ voice in my life?10
John 10:7-10
• The thief represented “all who came before” (v. 8) who claimed some authority to guide the sheep. What modern-day sources of authority tell people how to live and where to find pasture? In what ways is it accurate that the end result of such thieves’ voices is to “steal and kill and destroy” people’s lives?
• What would it look like for a Christian to allow Jesus to be the exclusive “door” for his life to keep “thieves and robbers” out?
“The thief comes only to steal and kill and destroy. I came that they may have life and have it abundantly. I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.”

John 10:10-15
• Reflect on the words that describe why Jesus came. In contrast to the thieves that come “only to steal and kill and destroy,” how does the gospel and following Jesus lead to an “abundant life”?
John 10:10-11, 14-15
• What are the characteristics of a good shepherd vs. a hired hand?
• Reflect on the fact that, as the good shepherd, Jesus has ownership over my life. To what extent have I yielded to his claim on my life?
John 10:17-18
• Reflect on v. 18 and what this means for me personally.

John 10:22-24
• Given that this happened during the Feast of Dedication, what might have motivated the Jews to ask the question about Jesus’ identity?
• Why would approaching Jesus as a potential political deliverer from the Roman occupiers eventually lead to anger and rejection? How does this dynamic play out in people’s approach toward God in our day?
John 10:27-29
• What are the characteristics of Jesus’ sheep?
• How do Jesus’ words—“no one will snatch them out of my hand”—encourage me in my struggles today?

John 10:31-42
• Jesus’ “many good works” were met with acceptance, while his claim to “make [himself] God” elicited a violent response. In what ways does this parallel people’s responses to Jesus today?
• Contrast the people’s response in vv. 41-42 with the response of the Jews in vv. 19-21, 24, 31-34, 39.
• What is behind this desire to separate Jesus’ good works from his claims about who he is?
• Are there some ways I can relate to the desire to separate aspects of Jesus that are widely accepted (his kindness,  high ethics, etc.) from his claims to divinity?

John 11:1-6
• What are some ways God has redeemed painful experiences in my life “for the glory of God, so that the Son of God may be glorified through [them]”?
• Are there seemingly hopeless situations in my life currently through which Jesus may be glorified?
John 11:14-15
• Jesus said he was glad at how events turned out “so that you may believe.” What can we learn about the nature of believing, given that Jesus was talking to his disciples who had been following him all along and already believed in him?
John 11:8, 12, 16
• The disciples seemed preoccupied with safety concerns (v. 8), and then misunderstood Jesus (v. 12) and the situation (v. 16). Think about the role of fear in affecting people’s perception of reality or in hearing God’s word accurately.  What are some fears that may be having a similar effect on my life right now?
“My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me. I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand.”

John 11:25
• Reflect on Jesus’ statement in v. 25. What are the implications of living with the belief that Jesus is “the resurrection and the life”?
John 11:33-36
• How did Jesus react when he saw Mary and the Jews weeping for Lazarus? What can we make of Jesus’ reaction,  given he already knew he would raise Lazarus from the dead (v. 11)?
• What common view of God does v. 35 challenge?

John 11:39-41
• Jesus told the people gathered there to “take away the stone.” How is Martha’s response relatable when thinking about what God can do in my life or in the lives of others?
• What lessons are there for me as I consider this passage?
John 11:43-44
• What two things did Jesus command, first to Lazarus and then to the others?
• How have I responded to both commands to “come out” and be “unbound”?

John 11:45-53
• What were the two drastically different responses of those who witnessed Jesus raising Lazarus from the dead?
• What was the real motivation behind the chief priests’ and Pharisees’ opposition to Jesus (v. 48)?
• To what extent is it true to this day that people resist Jesus mainly because he threatens their autonomy and security?
John 11:49-52
• Caiaphas the high priest unwittingly prophesied that Jesus “would die for the nation.” What can I learn about how God works from the fact that, even though Caiaphas was faithless, God was faithful to the office of high priest till the end?

• Reflect on Mary’s outpouring of this expensive perfume. In what ways was this appropriate given what Jesus was about to do (vv. 23-24)?
• What fundamental difference between Mary and Judas is revealed by his objection to what she did?
• Think about the progression of Judas’ actions: “he used to help himself” to the communal money and was now “about to betray [Jesus].”
• Judas expressed outrage in pretending to care for the poor. How might things have changed for Judas if he had been honest about what was going on in his heart?
Jesus said to her, “I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live.”

John 12:9-11, 17-19
• What lessons can I learn about human nature from the absurdity of the chief priests’ plan to put Lazarus to death?
John 12:12-19
• When the crowd cried out those words in v.13, what picture did they have in mind of who Jesus was and what he would do?
• What was the truth of who Jesus was and what he came to save people from? Are there ways I can relate to the crowd and their expectations of Jesus?
• When were the disciples able to understand fully all that was taking place?
• Why is the death and resurrection of Jesus the key to understanding the person of Jesus?

John 12:20-26
• Reflect on the link between glory and becoming “a grain of wheat [that] falls into the earth and dies” as it applies to Jesus’ life. How does the Christian understanding of glory differ from the world’s definition? In what ways have I embraced the life of becoming a grain of wheat that falls and dies?
• What are the ways in which people “lose” their life because they “love” it too much?
• In what ways am I challenged to use up my life rather than to cling onto it?
John 12:26
• Reflect on the call and promise contained in this verse. What is my response to this call and promise?

John 12:25-28
• Reflect on Jesus’ words: “But for this purpose I have come to this hour.” How does Jesus’ prayer demonstrate what it means to live out v. 25?
John 12:30-36
• Recall v. 24 and how Jesus refused to remain a single seed that does not die. How does Jesus willingly laying down his life cast out the “ruler of this world”?
• In what ways am I still listening to the “ruler of this world”?
• Reflect on how v. 32 has worked out in history and in my life.

John 12:37-40
• What was the reason the Jews could not believe in Jesus despite all the miraculous signs he had done?
• How can continuing to see the work of God and yet refusing to believe cause a person to become blinded and hardened?
John 12:42-43
• What was the dilemma for these leaders who would not confess their faith “for fear of the Pharisees […] so that they would not be put out of the synagogue”?
John 12:47-50
• What has been my attitude toward the word of God that “is eternal life” when believed, but which “will judge [me] on the last day” if rejected?

“Truly, truly, I say to you, unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit. Whoever loves his life loses it, and whoever hates his life in this world will keep it for eternal life.”

John 13:1-5
• What can we learn from the fact that Jesus chose this as his final lesson for his disciples?
John 13:6-9
• What may have been going on in Peter’s mind as he refused Jesus’ offer to wash his feet?
• What are some ways people respond to Jesus like Peter did? What can I learn about what it means to have a personal relationship with Jesus?
John 13:12-17
• How seriously have I taken the call to follow Jesus’ example of concrete humility and loving service for his disciples?  In what ways has following Jesus’ example led to blessing in my life?

John 13:21
• Reflect on the fact that Jesus was “troubled in his spirit.” How does this match or contrast with my view of Jesus?
John 13:27-30
• What may have been the heart of Jesus as he told Judas, “What you are going to do, do quickly”?
• Notice the progression from Satan prompting Judas (v. 2) to Satan entering into Judas (v. 27). What steps could Judas have taken to arrest the downward spiral of sin’s increasing hold over his will?

John 13:31-32
• How did God glorify Jesus through his voluntary death on the cross?
John 13:34-35
• What is the significance of this being Jesus’ final commandment to his remaining eleven disciples before his crucifixion?
• How does this clarify what the purpose of my life should be?

John 13:36-38
• Consider Peter’s heartfelt confession of devotion to Jesus. Did he mean it? What did Jesus know that Peter did not?
• How does the cross enable Peter, who would disown Jesus three times, to continue to follow Jesus eventually to his own death?
• Reflect on the frailty of human resolve, God’s generous heart, and our need for grace shown through this incident.

“A new commandment I give to you, that you love one another: just as I have loved you, you also are to love one another.  By this all people will know that you are my disciples, if you have love for one another.”

John 14:1-4
• Reflect on the personal way Jesus anchors his promise of heaven. What is the basis of my assurance that I will be with Jesus in the Father’s house?
John 14:5-7
• Reflect on v. 6. In what ways has Jesus been the way, the truth, and the life for me?
John 14:12-14
• What are the things I need to ask in Jesus’ name in prayer, and what are the “greater works than these” that I will do in order “that the Father may be glorified in the Son”?

John 14:15-26
• What is the relationship between knowing God, loving God, and obedience?
• What do I need to obey in order to grow in my knowledge of God in this season?
• What is the role of the “Helper”—“the Spirit of truth”—in a disciple’s life?

John 14:27-29
• Why was Jesus able to offer his peace to the disciples in the middle of telling them about his death?
• Contrast the peace that Jesus gives and the “peace” the world gives. How have I experienced this peace from Jesus?
John 14:30-31
• How is it that the ruler of this world has no claim on Jesus?
• What is the relationship between doing as “the Father has commanded me” and freedom from the influence of “the ruler of this world”?

42
John 15:1-8
• How is the relationship between the vine and the branch a particularly apt description of my relationship with God?  How is fruitlessness and withering away an accurate picture of a life that does not remain in Jesus?
• What is surprising about the fact that the vinedresser prunes the “branch that does bear fruit”?
• How does the word of God “prune” or “clean” a person?
• Notice that there is no command to bear fruit, but only the command to “abide in me.” What is the significance of this, and how does this apply to me?
“I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit, for apart from me you can do nothing.”

John 15:12-15
• Reflect on v.13 and the great love demonstrated by Jesus on the cross.
• What is the difference between a “friend” and a “servant”? What responsibility do I have since Jesus has “made known to [me]” “all that [he has] heard from [the] Father”?
John 15:12, 17
• Reflect on the repeated command to love one another.

John 15:18-21
• Why does the world hate Christians? How does this challenge my view of Christian life and my relationship with the world?
• What values do I hold that are “of the world”? What values do I hold that are not of the world?
John 15:22-27
• What made those who rejected Jesus guilty of sin? What responsibility do I have towards the word of God that I have heard and the works of God that I have witnessed?
• What is revealed about Jesus’ heart for the world, given the fact that he commanded his disciples to bear witness about him right after he told them the world would persecute them?

John 16:1-4
• Reflect on why Jesus “said all these things.” What is the role of Jesus’ words in keeping me steadfast?
• Reread John 13-15 with this in mind, and think about what lessons there are for me today.

John 16:8-11
• How have I experienced the Holy Spirit’s work of convicting me of sin, righteousness and judgment?
John 16:12-15
• What can I learn about Jesus from the fact that although he had so much to say to the disciples, he told them only what he knew they could bear?
• Are there situations where I need to trust that “when the Spirit of truth comes, he will guide [me] into all the truth”? How does this provide encouragement for the work God is asking us to do in ministering to people in all stages of their spiritual journey?
“This is my commandment, that you love one another as I have loved you. Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends.”

John 16:17-24
• In what ways do Christians today “weep and lament” while the world rejoices?
• How will our sorrow “turn into joy”? How is this joy unlike the world’s joy?
• What ought to be my perspective on present suffering?

John 16:31-33
• How could Jesus say, “Yet I am not alone,” in the face of the disciples’ imminent abandonment?
• Are there any situations in which I need to trust that “I am not alone, for the Father is with me”?
• What can I learn about Jesus from the fact that he told his disciples to “take heart” right after telling them about their impending failure and disloyalty?
• How do the cross and resurrection of Jesus allow me to “take heart” despite my failures?
• How does the triumph of Jesus help me to “have peace” in the midst of tribulations?

John 17:1-5
• What “authority over all flesh” has Jesus been granted?
• Notice Jesus’ definition of eternal life. In what ways can I pursue eternal life today?
John 17:1, 4-5
• What brings God glory? What specific work do I need to carry out on earth to bring God glory?

John 17:6-19
• What did Jesus pray for his disciples, and what can I learn from the fact that these are the issues he prayed about?
John 17:11, 14-18
• What are the unique challenges Christians face as those who are “in the world” but “not of the world”?
• How have I experienced the sanctifying work of God’s word in my life?
John 17:19
• How is Jesus’ love for his disciples manifested in this verse? For whose sake do I need to consecrate myself in order that “they also may be sanctified in truth”?
“I have said these things to you, that in me you may have peace. In the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world.”

John 17:20-23
• According to this passage, what is the basis and motivation for Christian unity?
• What is the relationship between oneness with God and oneness with the people of God?
• How does Jesus’ final prayer for his disciples show me what my priority should be? How can I grow in experiencing this kind of oneness with the people of God?

• What is revealed about Jesus’ heart in his prayer for his disciples to be with him in his glory?
• What assurance does this give me?
• How am I experiencing the process Jesus describes in v. 26?

John 18:1-8
• Reflect on how Jesus went out to meet the approaching soldiers “knowing all that would happen to him.” How does this contrast with my reaction when I need to move toward danger, discomfort or pain in the process of obeying God’s will?
• What must Jesus have been like given that those who came to arrest him “drew back and fell to the ground” upon meeting him?
John 18:11
• What does Jesus’ statement reveal about his posture toward “the cup that the Father has given [him]”?
• In what ways have I been (or am I being) tempted to “not drink the cup that the Father has given me”? Is there a “sword” Jesus calls me to put away, because I wield it to avoid the way of the cross?

John 18:15-18, 25-27
• Reflect on the picture of Peter warming himself by a fire while Jesus is inside being interrogated and beaten.  What does this reveal about human nature, and how does this apply to me?
• Are there some parallels between Peter’s denial of being associated with Jesus and the way I live?
John 18:19-24
• In what ways are people like the officer who struck Jesus?
• What can I learn from what Jesus says in v. 23 about how to respond to words spoken to me?

“The glory that you have given me I have given to them, that they may be one even as we are one, I in them and you in me, that they may become perfectly one, so that the world may know that you sent me and loved them even as you loved me.”

John 18:28
• Reflect on the irony of the Jews not entering the palace “so that they would not be defiled, but could eat the Passover” while they were bent on having Pilate kill an innocent man. What lessons or warnings might there be for me?
John 18:33-19:1
• What can I learn about Jesus’ heart through his interaction with Pilate?
• Contrast Jesus’ view of truth with Pilate’s view of truth as revealed by his action (19:1).

John 18:39-19:16
• How many times did Pilate declare Jesus’ innocence? Despite this, he ultimately hands Jesus over to be crucified.  Note the progression, and consider what Pilate forfeits in order to preserve his political position.
• In what ways have I seen this play out in our world today?
• What is the irony of Pilate saying, “Do you not know that I have authority to release you and authority to crucify you”? What can I learn about true power from Jesus’ response to Pilate?

tetelestai) signifies full completion of Jesus’ work and the establishment of a
• Reflect on Jesus’ final words: “It is finished.” What truths are contained in this final declaration?
• Reflect on the following passages in light of Jesus’ death on the cross.  What personal connections can I make?John 10:14–18 “I am the good shepherd. I know my own and my own know me, just as the Father knows me and I know the Father; and I lay down my life for the sheep. And I have other sheep that are not of this fold. I must bring them also, and they will listen to my voice. So there will be one flock, one shepherd. For this reason the Father loves me, because I lay down my life that I may take it up again. No one takes it from me, but I lay it down of my own accord. I have authority to lay it down, and I have authority to take it up again. This charge I have received from my Father.” John 12:27, 32“Now is my soul troubled. And what shall I say? ‘Father, save me from this hour’? But for this purpose I have come to this hour. … And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself.”

John 19:38-39
• What would have motivated Joseph of Arimathea to ask Pilate for Jesus’ body and Nicodemus to bring “a mixture of myrrh and aloes, about seventy-five pounds”?
• What was the significance of their actions?
• How do their actions show what my response to the cross should be?
“And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself.”

John 20:2-8
• What do the disciples demonstrate about the relationship between hearing, responding, and believing?
• Think about the process by which I came to believe in the resurrection of Jesus.
John 20:11-16, 19
• Contrast Mary with the disciples, who were huddled together with “the doors being locked…for fear of the Jews.” What made Mary so bold?
John 20:17
• What is amazing about Jesus calling his disciples his “brothers” after they had deserted him?
• What can I learn about the criteria for being part of God’s family and having the right to call God my Father and my God?

John 20:19-23
• What was the first responsibility that the risen Jesus gave to the disciples (vv. 22-23)?
• What has been my response to this calling?
John 20:24-29
• How did Jesus deal with Thomas’ doubt, and how did Thomas come to his confession that Jesus is indeed his Lord and his God?
John 20:30-31
• What was John’s purpose in writing the Gospel of John? What does it mean to have “life in his name,” and in what ways have I experienced that life?

John 21:1-3
• Why might Peter have gone back to fishing even after witnessing the resurrection?
• What is the significance of the disciples going back to fishing and catching nothing (cf. Mark 1:16-17)?
• What do I do when I experience disappointment or failure?
John 21:4-7
• What events have there been in my life of which I can say, “it [was] the Lord”? How does remembering my personal history bring clarity to my relationship with God?

John 21:15-17
• What can I learn about God’s perspective on my failures through Jesus’ interaction with Peter?
• What can I learn about God’s calling—“tend my sheep”—given that Jesus asks this of Peter, who had denied and failed him?
John 21:18-19
• How is the contrast between the time when one is “young” and “old” in v. 18 an apt description of Christian maturity? In what ways have I grown in allowing others to lead me where I do not want to go?
• Jesus commanded Peter, “Follow me,” which were the same words he used when he first called him to discipleship. How might these words have taken on a new and deeper meaning after everything that had happened?
• Are there ways that Jesus is asking me to follow him in a deeper way today?
But these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name.

• What are some recurring themes in Hannah’s prayer about who God is?
• Given what she believes about God, it makes sense that Hannah would rejoice upon dedicating baby Samuel to the Lord. What do I believe about God, and how does this affect how I feel about giving my time, resources, and energies to serving him?

1 Samuel 2:12-17
• Reflect on the brazen sinfulness of Eli’s sons. What makes their sin much more than personal moral transgression?
1 Samuel 2:22-36
• Even though Eli had reprimanded his sons, God still holds him responsible for his sons’ conduct. What lessons can I learn about fatherhood (parenthood) and about spiritual leadership?
1 Samuel 2:30, 35
• Reflect on what these verses show about the kind of person God is looking for.

1 Samuel 3:1
• What can I learn from the fact that “the word of the LORD was rare” under Eli’s leadership?
1 Samuel 3:9-12
• Reflect on the words: “Speak, for your servant hears.”
1 Samuel 3:13-14
• God holds Eli responsible for “the iniquity he knew” but did not stop.  What lessons about God-honoring relationships can I learn from this?
Therefore, having this ministry by the mercy of God, we do not lose heart.

1 Samuel 3:15-18
• Are there any situations where I am tempted to hold back from telling the truth?
• Reflect on Eli’s passivity regarding his sons and now God’s impending judgment. What lessons are there for me regarding my relationship with God and with others?
1 Samuel 3:1, 19-21
• During a very discouraging period in which the “word of the Lord was rare” (v.1) and the religious leaders were corrupt, how did Samuel’s life bring God’s presence back to Israel? Reflect on the importance of one person.

1 Samuel 4:1-5
• What are some ways in which people today might seek a sense of God’s presence or blessing in inauthentic or impersonal ways while ignoring the real state of their heart before God?
• Why would people do this, and why would this be offensive to God?
1 Samuel 4:11
• What can I learn from the fact that God allowed “the ark of God” to be captured by the Philistines?

• Eli’s sin as the spiritual leader of Israel wasn’t that he did not know what was right and wrong. It was that he did not take action according to his beliefs—in short, his sin was passivity. Reflect on the tragic outcome of Eli’s passivity. How was God’s name dishonored? Who are the people who suffered the consequences of his passivity?
• What warnings or lessons are there for me?

1 Samuel 5:1-8
• What assumptions about God and about Dagon were (or should have been) shattered by the events related in this passage?
• What can I learn about human nature from the fact that the Philistines’ reaction to the supernatural events surrounding the ark was simply to avoid further contact by sending it away?
1 Samuel 5:7-6:9
• Despite the evidence that it was the hand of God, why would they still consider the possibility that “it happened to us by coincidence”?

But we have renounced disgraceful, underhanded ways. We refuse to practice cunning or to tamper with God’s word,  but by the open statement of the truth we would commend ourselves to everyone’s conscience in the sight of God.

1 Samuel 7:3-6
• What are some components of repentance according to this passage?
1 Samuel 7:7-13
• Again, we see a biblical leader setting up a stone (v.12) as a memorial for what God had done. Reflect on your own “Ebenezer” times.
• What are some ways I can memorialize what God has done in my life?
1 Samuel 7:13-17
• Samuel continued to serve the people of Israel “all the days of his life.” Picture his life, his commitment, his endurance, and his love for God and His people. How does Samuel’s life challenge me as a follower of Christ?

1 Samuel 8:1-3
• Tragically, Samuel’s sons “did not walk in his ways. They turned aside after gain and took bribes and perverted justice.” The Bible shows no clear pattern of godly parents producing godly children (e.g., Saul/Jonathan, David/Absalom). What can be concluded about Samuel’s judgment at this point in his life from the fact that he “made his sons judges over Israel”?
1 Samuel 8:4-9
• What role did Samuel’s sons play in Israel asking for a king “like all the nations”? What lessons can I learn from this?
1 Samuel 8:5
• The Israelites wanted a king so that they could be “like other nations.” Reflect on the tragedy of this sentiment given the fact that their entire spiritual identity was that they were NOT like other nations, but rather a separate, called-out nation.
• To what degree have I embraced the call to be “called out,” and therefore different, from the culture around me?
Leviticus 18:1-4 (ESV)
”Speak to the You shall follow my rules and keep my statutes
Numbers 23:9 (ESV)
Psalm 106:35 (ESV)but they mixed with the nations and learned to do as they did.Ezekiel 20:32 (ESV)“What is in your mind shall never happen—the thought, ‘Let us be like the nations, like the tribes of the countries, and worship wood and stone.’”

1 Samuel 8:10-19
• Why would the Israelites still insist on having a king after being warned that their king would enslave them?
• What are some worldly sources people look to for deliverance, even after warnings or disappointment?
1 Samuel 8:7, 21-22
• What insight does this provide about the world and how God interacts with human decisions and events?

And even if our gospel is veiled, it is veiled to those who are perishing. In their case the god of this world has blinded the minds of the unbelievers, to keep them from seeing the light of the gospel of the glory of Christ, who is the image of God.

1 Samuel 9:16
• Reflect on the fact that though the Israelites reject God by asking for a king (1 Samuel 8:7), God chooses a king for them, remains responsive to their cries, and continues to work out his will (“save my people from the hand of the Philistines”) through the king.
• What hope does this give me in God’s willingness and ability to work out his will in my life despite my many past failures, sins, and mistakes?
• Recall situations in which God has worked in this way in my life, and pray a prayer releasing past sins and failures, and affirming God’s ongoing good work and guidance over my life.
1 Samuel 9:8-20
• Reflect on the fact that, in this chapter, Saul starts out on a mundane errand as a person of no standing. Yet, due to God’s choice of him as king, Saul is anticipated as a special guest, treated with honor by Samuel, and given a new identity. In what ways do these events parallel the work of the Gospel in my life?

1 Samuel 10:1-11
• What can I learn about God from the way he assures Saul of his calling?
• Reflect on how God has “turned [me] into another man” (v. 6).

1 Samuel 10:14-24
• How does Saul demonstrate his insecurity? Why is Saul’s insecurity inappropriate?
• What has been my response to God’s desire to use me?
1 Samuel 10:26-27
• Upon Saul’s confirmation as king, two groups of people emerge. On what basis does the second group reject Saul, and what does this reveal about their view of God? What effect would they have had on others, on Saul, and on God’s plan to deliver Israel from the Philistines through the new king?

1 Samuel 11:1-3
• Given the historical betrayal on the part of the people of Jabesh-gilead, what is notable about them sending out messengers throughout Israel?
• Are there ways I am reluctant to ask for help out of guilt from the past, or some complicated mixture of shame, awkwardness, expectation of rejection, or sense of resignation?
1 Samuel 11:1-7
• Contrast the two responses described in v. 4 and vv. 6-7.
• What can I learn from the fact that “they came out as one man” when “the dread of the LORD fell upon the people”?
For what we proclaim is not ourselves, but Jesus Christ as Lord, with ourselves as your servants for Jesus’ sake.

• What can I learn about human nature from the people’s reaction in v. 12?
• What can I learn from Saul’s response?
• How did God use this seemingly bad situation with Nahash the Ammonite for Saul’s good? What lesson can I learn about how to view challenges and difficulties?

1 Samuel 12:6-18
• What emotions were at play when the Israelites sought security outside of God?
• What was the crucial fact the Israelites forgot?
• What might be a modern-day Nahash for me and what do I need to do the next time I am confronted with such a situation?

1 Samuel 12:19-22
• What do we learn about God from this passage?
1 Samuel 12:20-25
• Reflect on Samuel’s exhortations to the Israelites in this passage.
1 Samuel 12:23
• Reflect on Samuel’s commitment to continue to pray for the people despite their repeated failures. In what ways have I been the recipient of this kind of commitment from God and from people that God has placed in my life?

1 Samuel 13:11-14
• It was a serious wrong for someone other than a priest to make burnt offerings. What drove Saul to do what he did? What does this reveal about Saul?
• What view of God do Saul’s actions reveal?
• What do I need to cultivate in order to not find myself in the same place as Saul?
For God, who said, “Let light shine out of darkness,” has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.

• Read this text with the following themes in mind:
• God’s presence/absence
• Bleak situations, obstacles
• Faith
• Few/many
• True partnership
• What do I need to learn from Jonathan? From the armor-bearer?

• How did people suffer under Saul’s impulsiveness?
• What warnings or lessons are there for me?
• What can I learn from the response of the men in v. 45?

1 Samuel 15:9
• What aspect of Saul emerges from v. 9?
1 Samuel 15:10-12
• Reflect on the picture of God and Samuel grieving for Saul, and the picture of Saul “[setting] up a monument for himself.” What explains this discrepancy?
1 Samuel 15:13-31
• Reflect on Saul’s resistance to admitting the true reason for his disobedience.  Why was he so reluctant?
• Reflect on the dissonance between v. 24 and v. 30.

1 Samuel 15:19-24
• What is the answer to Samuel’s questions in v. 19?
• What might be the relationship between v.17 (“little in your own eyes”) and v. 24 (“I feared the people”), and how do these themes work out in my life?
1 Samuel 15:34-35
• What is the significance of the prophet and king permanently separating?
• Reflect on Samuel and God’s response to Saul (v. 35).  Yet Saul has his own story.  What warning does this have for me?
But we have this treasure in jars of clay, to show that the surpassing power belongs to God and not to us.

Samuel 16:1-4
• What lesson can I learn about the place of grieving and the need to take action to advance God’s will?
• Is there some loss I am grieving or some discouragement I am dwelling on possibly to an inappropriate degree?
Samuel 16:6-7
he had no form or majesty that we should look at him,    and no beauty that we should desire him.He was despised and rejected by men,    a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief;and as one from whom men hide their faces         he was despised, and we esteemed him not. (Isa. 53:2b-3)
• Reflect on vv. 6-7. To what extent do I evaluate people based on external appearance or other such superficial features? How do these values play out in terms of how I see myself and others?

• Reflect on the words: “For the Lord sees not as man sees: man looks on the outward appearance, but the LORD looks on the heart.” How does this impact my life, on how I relate to God and to others?
• How can I cultivate this way of looking at myself and others?
• Considering that Jesse did not bother calling David to be present at the sacrifice, what assumption or bias caused Jesse to see David as an unlikely recipient of this anointing?
• Reflect on a time when God worked in my or another person’s life in ways that went beyond my assumptions.

• Reflect on the emotional state of Saul. After he fails to repent in Chapter 15, he’s never the same again. Reflect on Psalm 32 in this light.Psalm 32 (ESV), A Maskil Of David Blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven,whose sin is covered. Blessed is the man against whom the LORD counts no iniquity,and in whose spirit there is no deceit. For when I kept silent, my bones wasted awaythrough my groaning all day long. For day and night your hand was heavy upon me;my strength was dried up as by the heat of summer.  I acknowledged my sin to you,and I did not cover my iniquity;I said, “I will confess my transgressions to the LORD,”and you forgave the iniquity of my sin.  Therefore let everyone who is godlyoffer prayer to you at a time when you may be found;surely in the rush of great waters,they shall not reach him.

1 Samuel 17:11
• Contrast Saul’s response to Goliath to Saul’s response to Nahash the Ammonite in chapter 11. What happened to Saul?  What can I learn from this change?
• Recall the following words from the previous chapter: ”But the LORD said to Samuel, ‘Do not look on his appearance or on the height of his stature, because I have rejected him. For the LORD sees not as man sees: man looks on the outward appearance, but the LORD looks on the heart'" (1 Samuel 16:7). How does this theme of physical appearance play out in this chapter?
• How do I view people who are impressive in their abilities or accomplishments?
We are afflicted in every way, but not crushed; perplexed, but not driven to despair;

• Reflect on the contradiction between v. 20 and vv. 16, 24. What is the consequence of this gap between rhetoric  (“war cry”) and reality (“fled from him”)?
1 Samuel 17:27-29
• David’s brother rebukes him with an offensive slur on his character and motivation. What may have motivated such a sharp and unfair reaction? How do I respond to others when I am under stress or experiencing a 'shame storm'?

1 Samuel 17:34-37
• In what ways did David see the battle differently from Saul and the Israelites?  What enabled David to be so confident in God’s deliverance?
• Recall all the ways in which God has helped me grow in my trust in him.  Based on God’s faithfulness in his past dealings with me, in what areas can I learn to trust him more today?
1 Samuel 17:38-40
• David rejects the fine royal armor and chooses instead his staff, sling, and five smooth stones. What can I learn from this?

1 Samuel 17:41-58
• Think about why Goliath “disdained” David.
1 Samuel 17:45-47
• Meditate on David’s faith reflected in his words to Goliath.  How does the fact that “the battle is the Lord’s” encourage me to take steps of faith in my struggle against my fears?
• In light of the story's end, what can I conclude about the theme of God's presence/absence during the 40 days of Goliath's taunts?

1 Samuel 18:1-4
• Contrast the basis of Jonathan’s fondness of David with the usual reasons people feel fondness and closenesstoward others.
1 Samuel 18:5-9
• In what ways do I keep a jealous eye on others, making me unable to celebrate what God has done in and/or through them?
• How is jealousy of others a rebellion toward God?
• How else might Saul have responded to David?
persecuted, but not forsaken; struck down, but not destroyed; always carrying in the body the death of Jesus, so that the life of Jesus also may be manifested in our bodies.

1 Samuel 18:12-29
• Saul sees David as a threat and is therefore afraid of him. What perspective makes David a threat to Saul, and what perspective would make David a source of joy? What perspective do I have toward my brothers/sisters in Christ?
• What does Saul do with his fears, and what happens as a result?  What does this teach me about the operation of fear?

• Even though Saul agreed with Jonathan’s reasoning and even took an oath, his later actions expose the insanity of his jealousy.  How does this demonstrate the inadequacy of mere reasoning to uproot deeper sins of the heart?
• What drives people to take actions that defy what they know to be right and just?
• What can I learn about the strength of harbored sins and how I should deal with them?
• Is there some entrenched sin—jealousy, bitterness, resentment, or other stronghold—that I need to deal with more seriously than I have been?

1 Samuel 19:14-17
• Starting with Jonathan, and now Michal, Saul starts to lose his own family members. What warning does this give to me regarding what happens to a person who insists on acting out of jealousy or insecurity?
1 Samuel 19:18-24
• What can I learn from David’s decision to first go to Samuel in his time of fear and distress?
• What should Saul have recognized after “the Spirit of God came upon the messengers of Saul” for the third time? In what ways do people ignore God’s intervention while in pursuit of their personal agenda, and end up bringing shame upon their life?

1 Samuel 20:14-17
• What does Jonathan’s request to David reveal about what he believed about David’s future? How would this have strengthened David at this point in his life?
• Reflect on Jonathan’s relationship with David. In what ways can I learn to take more initiative in my relationships and become a source of encouragement and vision for my friends?
1 Samuel 20:30-34
• What warning does Saul’s anger toward Jonathan give me regarding my response when people do not agree with or cooperate with me?
1 Samuel 20:31, 35-42
• Reflect once again on Jonathan’s character, considering what Saul says (accurately) in v. 31. What must have been Jonathan’s view of God, history, and himself? How does this compare with how I view these issues?
For we who live are always being given over to death for Jesus’ sake, so that the life of Jesus also may be manifested in our mortal flesh. So death is at work in us, but life in you.

• Think about how David’s life has been after he was anointed by Samuel (chapter 16). What lesson can I learn from this?
• Spend some time reflecting on how God has helped me to grow through difficulties or long delays in the past.  What might God be teaching me through difficult circumstances today?
• While the situation may have appeared bleak, in what ways did God provide for David?  How have I experienced God’s timely provisions during difficulties?

• What can I learn from the fact that those who gathered around David were “everyone who was in distress, and everyone who was in debt, and everyone who was bitter in soul”?
• Reflect on the things David had to do during this time when he was on the run (chapter 21). How was God shaping David during this time as Israel’s future king? What perspective does this give me regarding times of personal difficulty and uncertainty?
• What can I learn about David’s faith in God during this uncertain time from the statement, “till I know what God will do for me”?

1 Samuel 22:6-8
• Why does it make sense that Saul—who has wronged David, rejected God, and refused to adjust to reality—winds up becoming paranoid?
1 Samuel 22:9-16
• Why would Saul (tragically) reject the very reasonable and plausible explanation of Ahimelech regarding his innocent provision for David?
• When I am faced with circumstances that cause me to suspect something, how ready am I to reject my own theory and believe the reasonable explanation of others?
• Which do I want to avoid more: being ‘duped’ or believing something false about someone?

1 Samuel 22:17-19
• Contrast Doeg the Edomite with the king’s officials who “would not put out their hand to strike the priests of the LORD.” What kind of person was Doeg the Edomite?
• Reflect on the progression of Saul’s jealousy towards David and the destruction it produced.
1 Samuel 22:20-23
• What can I learn about David from his response to Abiathar?

Since we have the same spirit of faith according to what has been written, “I believed, and so I spoke,” we also believe, and so we also speak, knowing that he who raised the Lord Jesus will raise us also with Jesus and bring us with you into his presence.

1 Samuel 23:1-12
• Given David’s men’s initial response and Saul’s statement, “he has shut himself in by entering a town that has gates and bars,” what must have been the military advisability of rescuing Keilah from the Philistines?
• What is remarkable about David’s concern for Keilah?
• What can I learn from the fact that God tells David to go and rescue Keilah even while David is on the run?
1 Samuel 23:7
• How is it possible for Saul to think that God is on his side in handing David over to him?  What is at the root of this kind of self-deception?

1 Samuel 23:12-18
• How might David have felt upon hearing that the citizens of Keilah would surrender him to Saul, and that he would once again be on the run?
• Think about the specific words Jonathan speaks to David.  What picture of encouragement can I find in this text?
1 Samuel 23:22-29
• How does this passage show that God is in control?
• Are there some temptations or difficulties I am facing right now where I need to trust God (cf. 1 Cor 10:13)?

• What about David’s personality is revealed from the fact that, though he did not intend to harm Saul, he still “stealthily cut off a corner of Saul’s robe”?
• Reflect on David’s amazing self-restraint. What non-negotiable principle must David have already adopted to be able to make such a quick and firm decision at a time when circumstances and the advice of his men seemed to all point in one direction?
• What spiritual and ethical non-negotiables have I adopted to guide me during pressing times when persuasive-sounding arguments and opportune circumstances can lead me astray?

• What can I learn from David’s speech to Saul?
• Despite his remorseful response to David, Saul later ends up hunting for David once again. In what ways do I respond with the right words or emotions to truths about myself, yet remain unchanged?
For it is all for your sake, so that as grace extends to more and more people it may increase thanksgiving, to the glory of God.

1 Samuel 25:1-3, 17, 25, 36
• Nabal’s folly might have been mitigated had he not been someone that “one cannot speak to.” How easy is it for people to talk to me about my character issues or blind spots?
1 Samuel 25:26-34
• What lessons can I learn from what Abigail says to David?
• What can I learn from David’s response to Abigail? Do I have the humility to abandon my course based on this kind of intervention?

1 Samuel 26:1-12
• Picture David hatching the plan to sneak into a camp of 3,000 enemy soldiers and then turning to the two men and saying, “Who will go down into the camp with me to Saul?”  What can I infer from this incident about the kind of leader he must have been?
• Although Abishai’s advice to David seemed logical, what can I learn from David’s response? What does this say regarding the importance of having a preset stance to be able to make a strong moral decision, even against the advice of those close to me?
1 Samuel 26:17-21
• Saul seems once again genuinely touched by David’s restraint and impressed deeply with the inevitability of David’s ultimate triumph. Yet he seems unable to act—namely, to surrender the throne—based on what he knows and feels deeply. What warnings or lessons can I learn from this?

• What are some words from v. 1 that tip us off to the source of David’s poor decision in fleeing to the Philistines?
• What is the irony of this after even his own enemy Saul blesses him (1 Samuel 26:25 - “Blessed be you, my son David! You will do many things and will succeed in them”)?
• What warning do I need to heed from this story?

1 Samuel 28:1-7
• Review how Saul was portrayed in 1 Samuel 11:1-11 and 1 Samuel 14:47-48 and then consider his reaction here in v. 5.  What is the relationship between Saul’s jealousy over David and the loss of the courage he once had as a valiant warrior?  What is the source of true courage?
• What can I learn about Saul (and human nature) from the fact that it was he who had expelled the mediums and spiritists from the land and yet he is now turning to one out of desperation?
• Are there illegitimate things that I turn to when I am overcome with fear and God seems to be silent?
So we do not lose heart. Though our outer self is wasting away, our inner self is being renewed day by day.

• What kind of dilemma did David’s shrewd manipulation of his enemies get him into?
• Reflect on how this entire distasteful chapter of David’s life began.  What lesson about trusting in God can I learn  from this?

• Why would David’s men think of stoning their leader when it was the Amalekites who carried off their families? What can I learn about human nature from this incident?
• Have there been times when I blamed others or became irrational because I was “greatly distressed” and “bitter in soul”?
• Contrast their response to the way David handles this situation.

1 Samuel 30:21-25
• What is the worldview of “the wicked and worthless fellows” who did not want to share the plunder with those who were “too exhausted” to continue to the battle?
• Contrast their response to David’s response in vv. 23-24. What is behind their different responses?
• Whose attitude can I relate to more? Do I hold to a view of ‘fairness’ that says people should get what they deserve?

1 Samuel 31:1-3
• Reflect on God’s hand in David’s life in helping him remain blameless towards Saul till the end.  What can I learn about God from the fact that he spared David from being part of this battle that is responsible for killing Saul and his sons, including Jonathan?
1 Samuel 31:1-10
• After years of rejecting God as king, Saul finally comes to a tragic end. What are the crucial steps that Saul took that led to this outcome of his life?
• How did Saul’s undignified death bring shame not only to his name but also to God’s name (vv. 9-10)?
1 Samuel 31:11-13
• What can I learn from the people of Jabesh-gilead about the proper place of gratitude and honor toward people based on their past conduct, even if their later conduct is not worthy of honor?

For this light momentary affliction is preparing for us an eternal weight of glory beyond all comparison, as we look not to the things that are seen but to the things that are unseen. For the things that are seen are transient, but the things that are unseen are eternal.
